## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

6ea82922  ₢A-AAv    Refactor diagnostic file naming to BCG-compliant per-call forensic preservation. Operator pointed out that ZBUJB_STEP4_STDOUT/STDERR were a single reused file pair that diag_dump cp'd out after each call — fragile under set-e abort (failing call's bytes survive but earlier successful calls' bytes are lost when abort fires before subsequent diag_dump cp), violates BCG line 525 (temp files preserved for forensic debugging) and BCG line 543-561 (auto-incrementing integer for uniqueness in loops, generalizing to cross-call sequential emission). New shape per BCG line 543 + 641 (mutable kindle state for counters): readonly ZBUJB_PLACE_TRUST_PREFIX (for zbujb_garrison_step4_place_trust call sites), readonly ZBUJB_VALIDATE_PREFIX (for zbujb_garrison_step6_validate call sites), and a SINGLE shared mutable kindle counter z_bujb_emit_index (lowercase per BCG mutability convention) that both wrappers increment — operator's framing 'use the same index mutable module variable for all emissions: we can see the resulting order'. Two parallel _run wrappers (zbujb_place_trust_run, zbujb_validate_run) bind their respective PREFIX to the shared counter and a printf-v zero-padded 2-digit index discriminator, append the caller-provided label, write to per-call stdout/stderr file pair, dump preview via the new generic zbujb_diag_dump_pair (replaces zbujb_garrison_step4_diag_dump — takes (label, stdout_path, stderr_path) args, no longer cps from a reused pair). Each wrapper captures the inner command's exit code via `\"\$@\" > out 2> err || z_exit=\$?` so set-e doesn't abort before diag_dump_pair runs, then `return \${z_exit}` propagates the failure for set-e at the caller's scope to handle (callers add `|| true` for tolerant probes; bare call for strict-fail). Net effect: each emission gets its own preserved file pair, file names sort lexically — `bujb_place_trust_00_mkdir_stdout.txt` through `bujb_place_trust_11_icacls-home-setowner_stdout.txt` then `bujb_validate_12_eventlog-operational_stdout.txt` etc. — embedded number reflects chronological emission order across BOTH originating functions, even though alphabetic sort groups by prefix. Call sites collapse from 3-line redirect+dump pattern to 1-line wrapper invocation. Mid-refactor over-consolidation incident: I initially collapsed both wrappers and prefixes into a single zbujb_emit_run + ZBUJB_EMIT_PREFIX, putting the originating-function name into the label as `place_trust__mkdir`-style. Operator caught and corrected: 'I didn't want you to consolidate the functions, just the index variable'. Reverted the consolidation; kept TWO prefixes + TWO wrappers + ONE shared counter. Operator note recorded: my Windows ACL / OpenSSH-Win32 StrictModes domain knowledge is shaky, multiple hypotheses about cause of bujuw_user silent rejection have been wrong (trailing newline, parent-dir StrictModes, home-dir StrictModes); current cycle adds the file-naming refactor that will make diagnostic forensics cleaner for whatever comes next. Test-suite re-run: skipped — refactor is additive instrumentation + helper extraction, doesn't change any tabtarget surface or existing test-suite-exercised path; bash -n green confirms syntax. Final cycle for this pace per operator framing: 'We won't have time to try any more cycles after this one'.            
a5a4526c  ₢A-AAv    Cycle 7: apply the home-directory lockdown repair indicated by cycle-6's smoking-gun Get-Acl finding. Cycle-6 evidence: `Get-Acl 'C:\\Users\\bujuw_user'` showed Owner=BUILTIN\\Administrators with inherited Everyone:RX + BUILTIN\\Users:RX entries from C:\\Users; no direct DACL entry for bujuw_user. OpenSSH-Win32's secure_permissions() walks the path-to-authorized_keys and rejects pre-auth (silent at default LogLevel INFO, the [preauth] close that surfaced this cycle) when any non-trusted principal has access — Everyone and Users are not in the trusted set by default. This is the cause of the workload round-trip failure, NOT a transport/content/file-ACL issue (those were verified correct by prelock-readback at 290B byte-for-byte match against curia-side ssh-keygen -y output). Repair: append two new icacls calls at the END of step 4 w-branch (after the existing .ssh-dir lockdown, before the case branch closes). Call 1: `icacls '${z_home_win}' /inheritance:r /grant 'SYSTEM:F' /grant 'BUILTIN\\Administrators:F' /grant '${z_wlu}:F'` — removes inherited entries from C:\\Users (eliminating the Everyone:RX and Users:RX entries that fail StrictModes), explicitly grants the trusted set: SYSTEM (sshd service account), BUILTIN\\Administrators (kept so admin can maintain the dir post-lock — different from .ssh which doesn't need admin retention), and the workload user themselves. Call 2: `icacls '${z_home_win}' /setowner '${z_wlu}'` — transfers ownership from BUILTIN\\Administrators to bujuw_user (some OpenSSH-Win32 versions require user ownership of own home; harmless if not). Each call wrapped per the established discipline: separate ssh round-trip, redirected stdout/stderr to ZBUJB_STEP4_*, diag_dump after with descriptive label (icacls-home-grant + icacls-home-setowner). Placement rationale: AFTER all .ssh-dir + file ops complete — admin retains WRITE_DAC on home via inherited entries from C:\\Users until /inheritance:r runs, so this is the last admin-side operation that needs home-dir access; nothing in steps 5-6 traverses the Windows-side home dir from the admin context. Order is intentional and stable: file ops -> file lockdown -> .ssh lockdown -> home lockdown -> step 5 (WSL-side privkey plant, doesn't touch Windows path) -> step 6 (workload SSH attempt — the test). All 8 cycle-6 diagnostic probes (5 in step 4 pre-lock, 8 in step 6 failure path) retained in case this fix is incomplete and we need another iteration. Operator framing recorded: probes-before-fixes paid off; cycle-6's empirical Owner+inherited-DACL data made this fix targeted rather than speculative; my track-record concern stands but this hypothesis has hard evidence behind it. Gates: bash -n green on bujb_jurisdiction.sh.

Six step-6-failure-path probes (added after the existing 5 from cycle 5), each `|| true` so any access-denied response surfaces as captured stderr without aborting the chain: (3) step6-getacl-home — `Get-Acl 'C:\\Users\\<wlu>' | Format-List | Out-String` — Get-Acl shows Owner directly (icacls doesn't), critical for distinguishing 'home dir owned by admin (StrictModes likely fails)' from 'home dir owned by bujuw_user'; (4) step6-getacl-dotssh — same on .ssh subdir, expected access-denied but might surface differently than icacls; (5) step6-getacl-authkeys — same on authorized_keys file; (6) step6-localuser — `Get-LocalUser '<wlu>' | Format-List | Out-String` to expose SID, Enabled, PrincipalSource etc — verifies the icacls grant principal resolves to the right SID and the user account isn't in some weird state; (7) step6-service-sshd — `Get-Service sshd | Format-List | Out-String` shows service status + StartType (didn't capture which account sshd runs as, but at least confirms the service is alive); (8) step6-sshdir-listing — `Get-ChildItem \$env:ProgramData\\ssh` lists files in the sshd config dir — surfaces whether file-based logging exists (logs/ subdir) that might have detail not in the Operational/Admin event channels. Repair: swap icacls call order in step 4 w-branch — file-level (grant + setowner) now precedes directory-level (grant + setowner). Last cycle's failure: dir-level /setowner to bujuw_user ran first, after which the file-level icacls call returned exit 5 (ERROR_ACCESS_DENIED) with stderr `C:\Users\bujuw_user\.ssh\authorized_keys: Access is denied.` — the privileged ssh session, having just transferred parent-dir ownership to bujuw_user and removed inheritance, lacked the inherited WRITE_DAC path it had been using to modify the child file's ACL (admin status + UAC-non-elevated-token interaction; the empirical fact is the access denied, the precise mechanism is academic). 6ea82922  ₢A-AAv    Refactor diagnostic file naming to BCG-compliant per-call forensic preservation. Operator pointed out that ZBUJB_STEP4_STDOUT/STDERR were a single reused file pair that diag_dump cp'd out after each call — fragile under set-e abort (failing call's bytes survive but earlier successful calls' bytes are lost when abort fires before subsequent diag_dump cp), violates BCG line 525 (temp files preserved for forensic debugging) and BCG line 543-561 (auto-incrementing integer for uniqueness in loops, generalizing to cross-call sequential emission). New shape per BCG line 543 + 641 (mutable kindle state for counters): readonly ZBUJB_PLACE_TRUST_PREFIX (for zbujb_garrison_step4_place_trust call sites), readonly ZBUJB_VALIDATE_PREFIX (for zbujb_garrison_step6_validate call sites), and a SINGLE shared mutable kindle counter z_bujb_emit_index (lowercase per BCG mutability convention) that both wrappers increment — operator's framing 'use the same index mutable module variable for all emissions: we can see the resulting order'. Two parallel _run wrappers (zbujb_place_trust_run, zbujb_validate_run) bind their respective PREFIX to the shared counter and a printf-v zero-padded 2-digit index discriminator, append the caller-provided label, write to per-call stdout/stderr file pair, dump preview via the new generic zbujb_diag_dump_pair (replaces zbujb_garrison_step4_diag_dump — takes (label, stdout_path, stderr_path) args, no longer cps from a reused pair). Each wrapper captures the inner command's exit code via `\"\$@\" > out 2> err || z_exit=\$?` so set-e doesn't abort before diag_dump_pair runs, then `return \${z_exit}` propagates the failure for set-e at the caller's scope to handle (callers add `|| true` for tolerant probes; bare call for strict-fail). Net effect: each emission gets its own preserved file pair, file names sort lexically — `bujb_place_trust_00_mkdir_stdout.txt` through `bujb_place_trust_11_icacls-home-setowner_stdout.txt` then `bujb_validate_12_eventlog-operational_stdout.txt` etc. — embedded number reflects chronological emission order across BOTH originating functions, even though alphabetic sort groups by prefix. Call sites collapse from 3-line redirect+dump pattern to 1-line wrapper invocation. Mid-refactor over-consolidation incident: I initially collapsed both wrappers and prefixes into a single zbujb_emit_run + ZBUJB_EMIT_PREFIX, putting the originating-function name into the label as `place_trust__mkdir`-style. Operator caught and corrected: 'I didn't want you to consolidate the functions, just the index variable'. Reverted the consolidation; kept TWO prefixes + TWO wrappers + ONE shared counter. Operator note recorded: my Windows ACL / OpenSSH-Win32 StrictModes domain knowledge is shaky, multiple hypotheses about cause of bujuw_user silent rejection have been wrong (trailing newline, parent-dir StrictModes, home-dir StrictModes); current cycle adds the file-naming refactor that will make diagnostic forensics cleaner for whatever comes next. Test-suite re-run: skipped — refactor is additive instrumentation + helper extraction, doesn't change any tabtarget surface or existing test-suite-exercised path; bash -n green confirms syntax. Final cycle for this pace per operator framing: 'We won't have time to try any more cycles after this one'.            
a5a4526c  ₢A-AAv    Cycle 7: apply the home-directory lockdown repair indicated by cycle-6's smoking-gun Get-Acl finding. Cycle-6 evidence: `Get-Acl 'C:\\Users\\bujuw_user'` showed Owner=BUILTIN\\Administrators with inherited Everyone:RX + BUILTIN\\Users:RX entries from C:\\Users; no direct DACL entry for bujuw_user. OpenSSH-Win32's secure_permissions() walks the path-to-authorized_keys and rejects pre-auth (silent at default LogLevel INFO, the [preauth] close that surfaced this cycle) when any non-trusted principal has access — Everyone and Users are not in the trusted set by default. This is the cause of the workload round-trip failure, NOT a transport/content/file-ACL issue (those were verified correct by prelock-readback at 290B byte-for-byte match against curia-side ssh-keygen -y output). Repair: append two new icacls calls at the END of step 4 w-branch (after the existing .ssh-dir lockdown, before the case branch closes). Call 1: `icacls '${z_home_win}' /inheritance:r /grant 'SYSTEM:F' /grant 'BUILTIN\\Administrators:F' /grant '${z_wlu}:F'` — removes inherited entries from C:\\Users (eliminating the Everyone:RX and Users:RX entries that fail StrictModes), explicitly grants the trusted set: SYSTEM (sshd service account), BUILTIN\\Administrators (kept so admin can maintain the dir post-lock — different from .ssh which doesn't need admin retention), and the workload user themselves. Call 2: `icacls '${z_home_win}' /setowner '${z_wlu}'` — transfers ownership from BUILTIN\\Administrators to bujuw_user (some OpenSSH-Win32 versions require user ownership of own home; harmless if not). Each call wrapped per the established discipline: separate ssh round-trip, redirected stdout/stderr to ZBUJB_STEP4_*, diag_dump after with descriptive label (icacls-home-grant + icacls-home-setowner). Placement rationale: AFTER all .ssh-dir + file ops complete — admin retains WRITE_DAC on home via inherited entries from C:\\Users until /inheritance:r runs, so this is the last admin-side operation that needs home-dir access; nothing in steps 5-6 traverses the Windows-side home dir from the admin context. 1	# jjezs_paddock ₣A-
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
6	tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.
7	Container runtime for release-1 is Docker Desktop for Windows only; the
8	WSL distro `rbtww-main` consumes Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL
9	integration rather than running its own dockerd. Native dockerd inside
10	WSL is named in §Deferred for the shared-kernel-iptables reason.
11	
12	## Current Concept
13	
14	**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
15	deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
16	its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module housing both verbs). Neither
17	the umbrella nor the implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing in
18	BUS0. Architectural spine in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.
19	
20	**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**
21	
22	- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
23	  rarely. Identifier: investiture.
24	- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Three fields:
25	  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
26	  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from privkeys via
27	  `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline strings.
28	  Identifier: investiture — by construction equal to a registered
29	  investiture. The BURP directory name must match a BURN profile dir;
30	  the 1:1 correspondence is enforced by file-presence check at load time.
31	
32	**BUJB constant:**
33	
34	- `BUJB_workload_user` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
35	  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.
36	  Lives in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` as a tinder constant.
37	
38	**Two verbs (Fenestrate, Garrison):**
39	
40	- **Fenestrate** — Windows-OpenSSH-only `sshd_config` harden plus admin SSH
41	  key-trust establishment. Uniform across shell-letters (no variant).
42	  Two-phase ceremony forced by `Restart-Service sshd` killing its own
43	  session. Tabtarget `buw-jpF` (capital F = persistent config application).
44	- **Garrison** — workload account provisioning only. Per-shell-letter
45	  (b/c/w). Never reads or writes any `sshd_config`; never alters admin
46	  authorized_keys. First action is admin SSH via key auth; failure surfaces
47	  immediately.
48	- For Linux/Mac there is no fenestrate verb — admin trust is operator-manual
49	  (e.g., `ssh-copy-id`); garrison runs against an existing key-trusted admin
50	  foothold. For Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening on Windows nodes (in addition to
51	  Windows OpenSSH), hardening is operator-manual.
52	
53	**Garrison-destructive model:**
54	
55	- Every garrison wipes prior workload state regardless of which investiture
56	  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
57	- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /
58	  vanquish / inventory verbs; garrison subsumes them.
59	- Admin-side sshd configuration is fenestrate's scope on Windows;
60	  operator-manual elsewhere.
61	
62	**Cross-cutting premises:**
63	
64	- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP reaches
65	  it by reference, never duplicates.
66	- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
67	  material; operator owns all key administration.
68	
69	**Tabtarget colophons (14 total; b/c/w shell support, PowerShell deferred):**
70	
71	- `buw-r[np][lrv]` — BURN/BURP regime config (List/Render/Validate) — 6
72	- `buw-jpF` — Fenestrate (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform) — 1
73	- `buw-jpG[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
74	  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
75	- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
76	- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1
77	- `buw-jws` — Interactive SSH as workload — 1
78	- `buw-hj0` — Handbook jurisdiction top — 1
79	
80	**Operational seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh`):**
81	
82	BCG-compliant module housing both fenestrate and garrison implementations,
83	hardcoding the three shell-letter→`command=` directive mappings (b/c/w)
84	used by garrison on the workload account, the workload privkey destination
85	path on remote per shell-letter, the canonical WSL distribution name
86	(`rbtww-main`), and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening directive
87	set used by fenestrate. SSH transport mechanics live in code; BURP carries
88	no shell selection.
89	
90	**Fenestrate ceremony (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform):**
91	
92	Two-phase remote-run because `Restart-Service sshd` terminates its own
93	ssh session.
94	
95	Phase 1 (over the password-or-key admin SSH session):
96	
97	1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
98	   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
99	   `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` authenticates by key. First-run: ssh prompts
100	   on /dev/tty; operator types the Windows admin password once. Fenestrate
101	   process never sees the password.
102	2. Idempotently place admin pubkey (derived from `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`
103	   via `ssh-keygen -y`) in `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare entry
104	   (no `command=`; fenestrate is uniform). Performed before any sshd_config
105	   change so key auth is available for phase 2's reconnect.
106	3. `icacls` lockdown on `administrators_authorized_keys`.
107	4. Write hardened directives to `sshd_config`: `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
108	   `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`. Idempotent.
109	5. Verify written state via PowerShell `Get-Content` returning raw bytes,
110	   then bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracting effective directive
111	   values. Keeps PowerShell out of parsing (its error semantics are
112	   unreliable).
113	6. `sshd -t` validates the config (penultimate atomic op of phase 1; a
114	   malformed config aborts fenestrate before it could brick running sshd).
115	7. `Restart-Service sshd` (final atomic op of phase 1; ssh session
116	   terminates here — bash orchestrator treats the disconnect as the
117	   expected phase boundary).
118	
119	Phase 2 (key auth only):
120	
121	8. Reconnect as `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
122	   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` (no password fallback). Failure
123	   here surfaces a brick — the operator's manual password no longer opens
124	   a session.
125	9. Verify post-state: the admin session authenticates by key alone and the
126	   running sshd serves the hardened configuration.
127	
128	**Garrison ceremony (per shell-letter; workload-only):**
129	
130	1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`; key auth
131	   only (no password fallback). On Windows, presupposes fenestrate has run
132	   for this investiture. On Linux/Mac, admin trust is operator-managed
133	   (e.g., `ssh-copy-id` placed the admin pubkey).
134	2. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or platform
135	   equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files outside home
136	   are workload's own concern, not garrison's.
137	3. Create fresh workload user named `BUJB_workload_user` (unprivileged: no
138	   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access).
139	4. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with the
140	   shell-letter's `command=` directive.
141	5. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded in
142	   `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can authenticate
143	   outbound to GitHub).
144	6. Validate round-trip — SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0.
145	
146	## Heat Sequence
147	
148	**Spec foundation:**
149	- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept
150	- introduce-fenestrate-narrow-garrison — split admin trust + sshd harden
151	  out of garrison into a uniform fenestrate verb
152	
153	**Implementation:**
154	- implement-jurisdiction — four-field BURP, `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`,
155	  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` module, 14 tabtargets, fenestrate + garrison
156	  ceremonies, cleanup superseded colophons + handbook entries
157	
158	**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**
159	- localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis
160	
161	**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
162	- WSL distribution install (`rbtww-main` set as default)
163	- Garrison-WSL exercise
164	- Garrison-Cygwin exercise
165	- Docker Desktop WSL integration enable + verify (no native dockerd)
166	
167	## Deferred (named for future return)
168	
169	- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpGp` tabtarget + the `p`
170	  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
171	  only.
172	- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
173	  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
174	- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
175	- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
176	  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
177	- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
178	  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
179	- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
180	- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.
181	- **Cygwin / WSL sshd hardening verb** — fenestrate covers Windows OpenSSH
182	  only. If c/w garrisons ever require their own sshd to be hardened
183	  programmatically (rather than operator-managed), that is a future
184	  fenestrate sibling.
185	- **Native dockerd inside `rbtww-main`** — Docker Desktop and a per-distro
186	  native daemon cannot safely coexist on the same Windows host: WSL2 distros
187	  share one network namespace, so two daemons fight for kernel iptables/NAT
188	  rules. Release-1 ships Docker Desktop only; Desktop's WSL integration
189	  serves the rbtww-main shells. If isolation needs ever require a native
190	  daemon, it would be operator-managed exclusive lifecycle (one daemon at
191	  a time, never both running concurrently).
192	- **JJK fundus user provisioning under WSL** — fundus is a JJK concept and
193	  currently deferred; the Windows handbook does not include a fundus phase.
194	
195	## Standing Notes
196	
197	- **Fenestrate handles first-time admin trust establishment on Windows.**
198	  On a fresh node, ssh's `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password` falls
199	  through to a /dev/tty password prompt; operator types the Windows admin
200	  password once. Fenestrate then installs the admin pubkey, sets the icacls
201	  ACL, hardens sshd_config (`PasswordAuthentication no`), restarts sshd,
202	  and reconnects to verify by key. Subsequent runs use key auth
203	  automatically. The operator's manual Windows scope reduces to: install
204	  OpenSSH server, enable the service, set `PasswordAuthentication yes`
205	  temporarily, allow the firewall port, ensure the admin user has a known
206	  password.
207	- **Linux/Mac admin trust is operator-managed.** No fenestrate verb;
208	  operator places admin pubkey via `ssh-copy-id` (or equivalent), and
209	  garrison's first admin SSH succeeds by key alone.
210	- **Two-phase fenestrate is structural, not optional.** `Restart-Service
211	  sshd` kills its own ssh session, so any post-restart verification must
212	  happen over a fresh session. Bash orchestrator treats the post-restart
213	  disconnect as the expected phase boundary, not a failure.
214	- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
215	  prompts interactively; fenestrate and garrison would hang. Operator
216	  policy: privkeys unencrypted; station-level security covers
217	  privkey-at-rest protection.
218	- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.** Operator
219	  pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal SSH key)
220	  before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a precondition.
221	- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
222	  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. If the current default WSL distribution differs,
223	  garrison-WSL fails fast with a clear error.
224	- **Docker Desktop is the release-1 container runtime on Windows.** WSL
225	  shells reach the daemon via Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL integration
226	  toggle (Settings → Resources → WSL Integration), enabled for `rbtww-main`.
227	  Cygwin and Windows-side shells reach the same daemon via the default
228	  Docker context. No separate dockerd inside the WSL distro; only one
229	  daemon ever runs concurrently.
230	- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
231	  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion and
232	  path construction.
233	- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
234	  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
235	- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
236	  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
237	- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite, GitHub,
238	  rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.
239	
240	# jjezs_pace ₢A-AAv
241	
242	## Character
243	Resume after WSG transport rules established; mechanical fix + verification cycle.
244	
245	## Docket
246	
247	Remaining work: step 5's `install: invalid user 'bujuw_user'` — body uses
248	plain `${ztmp}` and `$(mktemp)` which get eaten in transit (something
249	between cmd.exe and Linux bash via wsl.exe). Prior cycles landed: channel
250	rewrite (heredoc → args form), obliterate orphan-purge + userdel reorder,
251	wrapper $LASTEXITCODE init repair, BCG-compliant transport (no $()/pipeline
252	in module code, no remote temp file). Garrison-w on bujn-winpc clears
253	obliterate, step 3 (useradd persists in /etc/passwd with bujuw_user-owned
254	home + skel copied), and step 4 (Windows-side authorized_keys placed).
255	Step 5 is the lone remaining failure.
256	
257	Locked decisions:
258	
259	- Read WSG (Tools/buk/vov_veiled/WSG-WindowsScriptingGuide.md) before
260	  mount. WSG's body-side escape rule (formerly OQ-4, established by the
261	  experiment pace) is canonical — apply it to bodies, choosing the layer
262	  (caller-side per-arg escape, or zbujb_admin_exec doing the escape
263	  internally) per WSG's recommendation. Future Windows-transport bodies
264	  follow the same rule.
265	- Step 5 b|w branch is the only current body with $ references that need
266	  the escape. Steps 3 and 4 (w branch) have no $ references and worked
267	  in cycle 3 unmodified — leave alone unless WSG-OQ4 dictates otherwise.
268	- Diagnostic instrumentation in zbujb_obliterate_windows_namespaces — the
269	  per-step diag_dump helper and baseline Get-Date probe added during the
270	  diagnostic cycles — remains in place. Pace-close decision: keep with a
271	  doc-comment naming it forensic, or prune (remove helper + 11 call
272	  sites). Operator preference at close.
273	
274	Done when:
275	- bash -n green on bujb_jurisdiction.sh
276	- tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green
277	- tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh green at baseline
278	- tt/buw-jpGw.GarrisonWsl.sh bujn-winpc runs end-to-end through all 6
279	  steps including step-6 round-trip knock, with workload identity
280	  bujuw_user
281	- BUSJGW reads coherently against implemented behavior
282	- No BURC_WORKLOAD_USER references remain (verified clean in cycle 1;
283	  preserve)
284	- Diag_dump instrumentation decision recorded (kept-with-note or pruned)
285

# jjezs_paddock ₣A-

## Goal

Windows test infrastructure for Recipe Bottle. Run RB's container isolation
tests on a Windows host with WSL, Cygwin, and PowerShell access paths.
Container runtime for release-1 is Docker Desktop for Windows only; the
WSL distro `rbtww-main` consumes Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL
integration rather than running its own dockerd. Native dockerd inside
WSL is named in §Deferred for the shared-kernel-iptables reason.

## Current Concept

**Jurisdiction** is the BUK feature-area for reaching out to remote nodes
deterministically. Logical-only umbrella concept; `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` is
its implementation seat (BCG-compliant module housing both verbs). Neither
the umbrella nor the implementation module earns AXLA-entity voicing in
BUS0. Architectural spine in BUS0 §Remote Node Access.

**Two regimes (BURN, BURP):**

- BURN — node-shape (host, OS family). Per-node, git-transported, changes
  rarely. Identifier: investiture.
- BURP — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user. Three fields:
  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`,
  `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`. Pubkeys derived from privkeys via
  `ssh-keygen -y` at use-time; no separate pubkey paths or inline strings.
  Identifier: investiture — by construction equal to a registered
  investiture. The BURP directory name must match a BURN profile dir;
  the 1:1 correspondence is enforced by file-presence check at load time.

**BUJB constant:**

- `BUJB_workload_user` — project-wide convention name for the workload OS
  user that garrison provisions on every node. Single source of truth.
  Lives in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` as a tinder constant.

**Two verbs (Fenestrate, Garrison):**

- **Fenestrate** — Windows-OpenSSH-only `sshd_config` harden plus admin SSH
  key-trust establishment. Uniform across shell-letters (no variant).
  Two-phase ceremony forced by `Restart-Service sshd` killing its own
  session. Tabtarget `buw-jpF` (capital F = persistent config application).
- **Garrison** — workload account provisioning only. Per-shell-letter
  (b/c/w). Never reads or writes any `sshd_config`; never alters admin
  authorized_keys. First action is admin SSH via key auth; failure surfaces
  immediately.
- For Linux/Mac there is no fenestrate verb — admin trust is operator-manual
  (e.g., `ssh-copy-id`); garrison runs against an existing key-trusted admin
  foothold. For Cygwin/WSL sshd hardening on Windows nodes (in addition to
  Windows OpenSSH), hardening is operator-manual.

**Garrison-destructive model:**

- Every garrison wipes prior workload state regardless of which investiture
  or operator runs it. Node runtime state belongs to whoever last garrisoned.
- One workload user per node, ever. No conscript / discharge / withdraw /
  vanquish / inventory verbs; garrison subsumes them.
- Admin-side sshd configuration is fenestrate's scope on Windows;
  operator-manual elsewhere.

**Cross-cutting premises:**

- `burn_host_singularity` — host of a node lives only in BURN; BURP reaches
  it by reference, never duplicates.
- `bus_keys_operator_owned` — system never generates or modifies SSH key
  material; operator owns all key administration.

**Tabtarget colophons (14 total; b/c/w shell support, PowerShell deferred):**

- `buw-r[np][lrv]` — BURN/BURP regime config (List/Render/Validate) — 6
- `buw-jpF` — Fenestrate (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform) — 1
- `buw-jpG[bcw]` — Garrison per shell-letter (b=native bash for Linux/Mac;
  c=Cygwin; w=WSL) — 3
- `buw-jwk` — Knock (probe workload reachability) — 1
- `buw-jwc` — Run command file as workload (shell determined by garrison) — 1
- `buw-jws` — Interactive SSH as workload — 1
- `buw-hj0` — Handbook jurisdiction top — 1

**Operational seat (`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` + `bujb_cli.sh`):**

BCG-compliant module housing both fenestrate and garrison implementations,
hardcoding the three shell-letter→`command=` directive mappings (b/c/w)
used by garrison on the workload account, the workload privkey destination
path on remote per shell-letter, the canonical WSL distribution name
(`rbtww-main`), and the Windows OpenSSH `sshd_config` hardening directive
set used by fenestrate. SSH transport mechanics live in code; BURP carries
no shell selection.

**Fenestrate ceremony (Windows OpenSSH only; uniform):**

Two-phase remote-run because `Restart-Service sshd` terminates its own
ssh session.

Phase 1 (over the password-or-key admin SSH session):

1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password`. Steady-state:
   `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE` authenticates by key. First-run: ssh prompts
   on /dev/tty; operator types the Windows admin password once. Fenestrate
   process never sees the password.
2. Idempotently place admin pubkey (derived from `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`
   via `ssh-keygen -y`) in `administrators_authorized_keys` as a bare entry
   (no `command=`; fenestrate is uniform). Performed before any sshd_config
   change so key auth is available for phase 2's reconnect.
3. `icacls` lockdown on `administrators_authorized_keys`.
4. Write hardened directives to `sshd_config`: `PubkeyAuthentication yes`,
   `PasswordAuthentication no`, `PermitEmptyPasswords no`. Idempotent.
5. Verify written state via PowerShell `Get-Content` returning raw bytes,
   then bash-side BCG-compliant matching extracting effective directive
   values. Keeps PowerShell out of parsing (its error semantics are
   unreliable).
6. `sshd -t` validates the config (penultimate atomic op of phase 1; a
   malformed config aborts fenestrate before it could brick running sshd).
7. `Restart-Service sshd` (final atomic op of phase 1; ssh session
   terminates here — bash orchestrator treats the disconnect as the
   expected phase boundary).

Phase 2 (key auth only):

8. Reconnect as `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with
   `PreferredAuthentications=publickey` (no password fallback). Failure
   here surfaces a brick — the operator's manual password no longer opens
   a session.
9. Verify post-state: the admin session authenticates by key alone and the
   running sshd serves the hardened configuration.

**Garrison ceremony (per shell-letter; workload-only):**

1. SSH-as-`BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` with `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`; key auth
   only (no password fallback). On Windows, presupposes fenestrate has run
   for this investiture. On Linux/Mac, admin trust is operator-managed
   (e.g., `ssh-copy-id` placed the admin pubkey).
2. Destroy existing workload user (if present) via `userdel -r` or platform
   equivalent — removes account + home directory; stray files outside home
   are workload's own concern, not garrison's.
3. Create fresh workload user named `BUJB_workload_user` (unprivileged: no
   sudo, no admin group, ssh-only access).
4. Place workload pubkey in workload's authorized_keys with the
   shell-letter's `command=` directive.
5. Copy workload privkey to remote workload account at the path hardcoded in
   `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` per shell-letter (so workload can authenticate
   outbound to GitHub).
6. Validate round-trip — SSH as workload + exec no-op + verify exit 0.

## Heat Sequence

**Spec foundation:**
- spec-bus0-jurisdiction — BUS0 rewrite reflecting current concept
- introduce-fenestrate-narrow-garrison — split admin trust + sshd harden
  out of garrison into a uniform fenestrate verb

**Implementation:**
- implement-jurisdiction — four-field BURP, `BURC_WORKLOAD_USER`,
  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh` module, 14 tabtargets, fenestrate + garrison
  ceremonies, cleanup superseded colophons + handbook entries

**Investigation (independent, runs anytime):**
- localhost-fundus-parallel-saturation diagnosis

**Practice (Windows host, blocks on implementation):**
- WSL distribution install (`rbtww-main` set as default)
- Garrison-WSL exercise
- Garrison-Cygwin exercise
- Docker Desktop WSL integration enable + verify (no native dockerd)

## Deferred (named for future return)

- **PowerShell garrison + dispatch** — `buw-jpGp` tabtarget + the `p`
  shell-letter mapping in `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. Current heat ships b/c/w
  only.
- **Adopt** — cross-station-user pubkey install via existing privileged trust.
  Today: each station user runs the ceremony fresh from their own machine.
- **User regime** — centralized per-user pubkey for shared admin scenarios.
- **Separate workload→GitHub keypair** — current shape reuses workload SSH
  keypair for outbound; tighten to per-purpose separation if security demands.
- **BURP override fields for non-conventional key layouts** — operators with
  centralized SSH key storage, non-`.pub`-suffix layouts.
- **Localhost garrison/dispatch variants** — deferred; remote nodes only.
- **Git normalization on remote** — JJK has `jjx_plant`; BUK doesn't duplicate.
- **Cygwin / WSL sshd hardening verb** — fenestrate covers Windows OpenSSH
  only. If c/w garrisons ever require their own sshd to be hardened
  programmatically (rather than operator-managed), that is a future
  fenestrate sibling.
- **Native dockerd inside `rbtww-main`** — Docker Desktop and a per-distro
  native daemon cannot safely coexist on the same Windows host: WSL2 distros
  share one network namespace, so two daemons fight for kernel iptables/NAT
  rules. Release-1 ships Docker Desktop only; Desktop's WSL integration
  serves the rbtww-main shells. If isolation needs ever require a native
  daemon, it would be operator-managed exclusive lifecycle (one daemon at
  a time, never both running concurrently).
- **JJK fundus user provisioning under WSL** — fundus is a JJK concept and
  currently deferred; the Windows handbook does not include a fundus phase.

## Standing Notes

- **Fenestrate handles first-time admin trust establishment on Windows.**
  On a fresh node, ssh's `PreferredAuthentications=publickey,password` falls
  through to a /dev/tty password prompt; operator types the Windows admin
  password once. Fenestrate then installs the admin pubkey, sets the icacls
  ACL, hardens sshd_config (`PasswordAuthentication no`), restarts sshd,
  and reconnects to verify by key. Subsequent runs use key auth
  automatically. The operator's manual Windows scope reduces to: install
  OpenSSH server, enable the service, set `PasswordAuthentication yes`
  temporarily, allow the firewall port, ensure the admin user has a known
  password.
- **Linux/Mac admin trust is operator-managed.** No fenestrate verb;
  operator places admin pubkey via `ssh-copy-id` (or equivalent), and
  garrison's first admin SSH succeeds by key alone.
- **Two-phase fenestrate is structural, not optional.** `Restart-Service
  sshd` kills its own ssh session, so any post-restart verification must
  happen over a fresh session. Bash orchestrator treats the post-restart
  disconnect as the expected phase boundary, not a failure.
- **Encrypted (passphrase-protected) privkeys not supported.** `ssh-keygen -y`
  prompts interactively; fenestrate and garrison would hang. Operator
  policy: privkeys unencrypted; station-level security covers
  privkey-at-rest protection.
- **Workload pubkey must be pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band.** Operator
  pastes the pubkey into GitHub (deploy key per repo or personal SSH key)
  before garrison runs. Garrison cannot validate this; it is a precondition.
- **WSL distribution is hardcoded to `rbtww-main`** in
  `bujb_jurisdiction.sh`. If the current default WSL distribution differs,
  garrison-WSL fails fast with a clear error.
- **Docker Desktop is the release-1 container runtime on Windows.** WSL
  shells reach the daemon via Docker Desktop's per-distro WSL integration
  toggle (Settings → Resources → WSL Integration), enabled for `rbtww-main`.
  Cygwin and Windows-side shells reach the same daemon via the default
  Docker context. No separate dockerd inside the WSL distro; only one
  daemon ever runs concurrently.
- **Some `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER` values may contain spaces** (e.g., Windows
  admin user `b hyslop`); audit shell quoting at every variable expansion and
  path construction.
- Tailscale provides stable transport (Mac sees `rocket` as `100.x.y.z` /
  tailnet hostname); not adopted as a BUK dependency.
- PowerShell-from-WSL pattern: bash owns control flow, PowerShell is the
  Windows syscall layer; one atomic command per call.
- `command=` in authorized_keys is OpenSSH-standard (used by gitolite, GitHub,
  rsync, borg); shrinks key blast radius rather than expanding it.

file.content:
# This is the sshd server system-wide configuration file.  See
# sshd_config(5) for more information.

# The strategy used for options in the default sshd_config shipped with
# OpenSSH is to specify options with their default value where
# possible, but leave them commented.  Uncommented options override the
# default value.

#Port 22
#AddressFamily any
#ListenAddress 0.0.0.0
#ListenAddress ::

#HostKey __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/ssh_host_rsa_key
#HostKey __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/ssh_host_dsa_key
#HostKey __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key
#HostKey __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/ssh_host_ed25519_key

# Ciphers and keying
#RekeyLimit default none

# Logging
#SyslogFacility AUTH
#LogLevel INFO

# Authentication:

#LoginGraceTime 2m
#PermitRootLogin prohibit-password
#StrictModes yes
#MaxAuthTries 6
#MaxSessions 10

PubkeyAuthentication yes

# The default is to check both .ssh/authorized_keys and .ssh/authorized_keys2
# but this is overridden so installations will only check .ssh/authorized_keys
AuthorizedKeysFile	.ssh/authorized_keys

#AuthorizedPrincipalsFile none

# For this to work you will also need host keys in %programData%/ssh/ssh_known_hosts
#HostbasedAuthentication no
# Change to yes if you don't trust ~/.ssh/known_hosts for
# HostbasedAuthentication
#IgnoreUserKnownHosts no
# Don't read the user's ~/.rhosts and ~/.shosts files
#IgnoreRhosts yes

# To disable tunneled clear text passwords, change to no here!
PasswordAuthentication no
PermitEmptyPasswords no

# GSSAPI options
#GSSAPIAuthentication no

#AllowAgentForwarding yes
#AllowTcpForwarding yes
#GatewayPorts no
#PermitTTY yes
#PrintMotd yes
#PrintLastLog yes
#TCPKeepAlive yes
#UseLogin no
#PermitUserEnvironment no
#ClientAliveInterval 0
#ClientAliveCountMax 3
#UseDNS no
#PidFile /var/run/sshd.pid
#MaxStartups 10:30:100
#PermitTunnel no
#ChrootDirectory none
#VersionAddendum none

# no default banner path
#Banner none

# override default of no subsystems
Subsystem	sftp	sftp-server.exe

# Example of overriding settings on a per-user basis
#Match User anoncvs
#	AllowTcpForwarding no
#	PermitTTY no
#	ForceCommand cvs server

Match Group administrators
       AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/administrators_authorized_keys

content.text:
**Result: cycle-7 home-dir lockdown landed correctly but step 6 still fails. New ground truth captured.**

**Verified by the run:**
- Home dir `C:\Users\bujuw_user`: Owner=`rocket\bujuw_user`, ACL=`SYSTEM:F + Administrators:F + bujuw_user:F` (the inherited `Everyone:RX` and `Users:RX` from cycle-6 are gone).
- `.ssh` and `authorized_keys`: Owner=`bujuw_user`, ACL=`SYSTEM:F + bujuw_user:F` only — admin shut out, exactly the StrictModes-canonical shape.
- bujuw_user exists, Enabled=True, SID `S-1-5-21-3276480634-3513337142-777477859-1020`, PrincipalSource=Local.
- sshd_config: `PubkeyAuthentication yes`, no `AllowUsers/DenyUsers`, default `AuthorizedKeysFile .ssh/authorized_keys`.

**Smoking gun in `eventlog-operational` (this run captured what cycles 5–7 missed):**
```
2:11:02 PM  sshd: Connection closed by authenticating user bujuw_user 100.100.223.55 port 49276 [preauth]
```
No `Failed publickey` line precedes it. sshd dropped the connection before any auth method evaluated the offered key — i.e., before consulting `authorized_keys`. So home-dir StrictModes was NOT the gating issue. The fix was correct in shape, but not the cause.

**The most likely remaining cause** (from sshd_config lines 87–88):
```
Match Group administrators
       AuthorizedKeysFile __PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/administrators_authorized_keys
```
This is the OpenSSH-Win32 default Match block. **If `bujuw_user` is somehow a member of the local `Administrators` group**, sshd reads `administrators_authorized_keys` (102B — bhyslop's admin key only) and never looks at `.ssh/authorized_keys`. With no acceptable key offered, sshd closes preauth with no "Failed publickey" line — exactly the shape we're seeing. None of our probes capture group membership; `Get-LocalUser` doesn't show it.

**Other hypotheses (less likely):**
- `secure_permissions()` failing on `C:\Users` itself (parent of locked home) — but bhyslop's `__PROGRAMDATA__\ssh\administrators_authorized_keys` path walks through `C:\ProgramData → C:\` which has similar inherited entries and works fine.
- LogLevel is default INFO; `VERBOSE` or `DEBUG3` would surface the actual decision sshd made — but that change is operator territory and out of scope per the no-more-cycles directive.

**Per operator framing — stopping here without further cycles.** Recommendation for the next pace (when picked up):
1. **Cheap decisive probe (one round-trip, no fix):** `Get-LocalGroupMember -Group Administrators` and `Get-LocalGroupMember -Group Users` for `bujuw_user`. If admin membership is present, root cause is identified — fix is in step 3 (`New-LocalUser` + explicit `Remove-LocalGroupMember -Group Administrators`, or just confirm Users-only).
2. **If not group-membership:** elevate LogLevel to `DEBUG3` in `sshd_config`, restart sshd via fenestrate, retry knock — sshd will log the exact reason for the preauth close (StrictModes path-walk failure, key-not-acceptable, account-policy, etc).
3. **File-based sshd logs:** `C:\ProgramData\ssh\logs\` exists as a directory. Worth probing whether `SyslogFacility LOCAL0` plus `LogLevel VERBOSE` in sshd_config produces text logs there with more detail than the Windows event log.

**Pace state:** the cycle-7 fix is on disk and correct in shape (home-dir lockdown is the right thing to do for OpenSSH-Win32 even if it didn't solve the gating issue). Cycle-8's diagnostic-naming refactor is also additive and useful. Both should be kept regardless of what the next pace does.

**Wrap-decision territory (operator):** options I see, ordered by my preference —
1. **Wrap as-is with summary noting the new ground truth** (preauth close captured, hypothesis: admin group membership, next-pace probe identified). Lets the heat advance; the next pace picks up cleanly.
2. **Reslate the docket** to reflect step-6 reality + group-membership hypothesis, leave pace open, mount fresh in a new session.
3. **Drop the pace and split** — original silks `correct-wsl-user-model` is now wholly stale; a new pace `garrison-wsl-step6-roundtrip` cleanly framed around the [preauth] close would be more honest.

Awaiting direction; not running anything else.

input.prompt:
Search the web and synthesize findings on a specific OpenSSH-on-Windows symptom. Keep the result under 400 words. I need actionable diagnostic next-steps, not background lecture.

**Symptom:** OpenSSH server on Windows silently rejects a non-admin local user's public-key authentication. Client sees `Permission denied (publickey,keyboard-interactive)` exit 255. Server's OpenSSH/Operational event log shows:
```
sshd: Connection closed by authenticating user bujuw_user 100.100.223.55 port 49276 [preauth]
```
No `Failed publickey` entry precedes it. sshd is dropping the connection BEFORE evaluating the offered key.

**State verified by probes (so we can rule these out):**
- User exists, Enabled=True, PrincipalSource=Local, has SID 1020 (regular non-admin range).
- `C:\Users\bujuw_user`: Owner=user, ACL=`SYSTEM:F + Administrators:F + user:F` (no Everyone, no Users).
- `C:\Users\bujuw_user\.ssh`: Owner=user, ACL=`SYSTEM:F + user:F` only.
- `C:\Users\bujuw_user\.ssh\authorized_keys`: Owner=user, ACL=`SYSTEM:F + user:F` only.
- sshd_config has `PubkeyAuthentication yes`, `PasswordAuthentication no`, default `AuthorizedKeysFile .ssh/authorized_keys`, no `AllowUsers/DenyUsers/AllowGroups/DenyGroups`.
- sshd_config has the default `Match Group administrators` block redirecting authorized_keys to `__PROGRAMDATA__/ssh/administrators_authorized_keys` for admin group members.
- Same sshd happily accepts publickey auth for the admin user (bhyslop) using `administrators_authorized_keys`.
- LogLevel is default INFO.
- A `logs` directory exists under `C:\ProgramData\ssh\`.

**The specific questions I need answered:**
1. What are the known causes of `Connection closed ... [preauth]` on OpenSSH-Win32 when no `Failed publickey` precedes it? Rank by likelihood given the verified state above.
2. Does OpenSSH-Win32's `secure_permissions()` walk parent directories above the home dir (e.g., `C:\Users`, `C:\`)? If so, what ACL state on those parents would cause silent rejection?
3. Is the `Match Group administrators` block known to silently apply to non-admin users in any edge case (e.g., user accidentally in admins group, SID-based vs name-based group resolution quirks)?
4. How do you enable `LogLevel DEBUG3` and file-based logging in OpenSSH-Win32 (`SyslogFacility LOCAL0`) — and where do the logs land?
5. Are there cheap one-shot probes (PowerShell or icacls one-liners) that decisively diagnose this case without restarting sshd or modifying state?

Search with year 2025/2026. Prefer Microsoft docs (learn.microsoft.com), the openssh-portable PowerShell-Win32 fork (github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH), and well-known Windows-admin sources. Skip Stack Overflow answers older than 2022.

Output format: numbered answers to the 5 questions, terse. End with a "Recommended next probes" list of 3-5 specific commands.

content:
<task-notification>
<task-id>a5e9f897d281d28ed</task-id>
<tool-use-id>toolu_01388vhUa8g7cmjEhT3sRzo7</tool-use-id>
<output-file>/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-bhyslop-projects-rbm-alpha-recipemuster/79e93d40-684a-4a38-a528-fe3e0738f3f7/tasks/a5e9f897d281d28ed.output</output-file>
<status>completed</status>
<summary>Agent "Research OpenSSH-Win32 preauth close" completed</summary>
<result>Researching OpenSSH-Win32 silent preauth-close on non-admin local user with verified-clean ACLs.

**1. Causes ranked by likelihood given verified state:**
- **(High) sshd service-account cannot read the file.** Issue #1450 documents this exactly: "Won't read USER/.ssh/authorized_keys if permissions [omit] the sshd service user." Default sshd install runs as `LocalSystem` (= SYSTEM, which IS in your ACL), but newer/hardened setups run sshd under the virtual account `NT SERVICE\sshd` — distinct from SYSTEM. With your `SYSTEM:F + user:F` lockdown, if sshd runs as `NT SERVICE\sshd`, it has no access path to read the file, and the connection closes silently at preauth before logging "Failed publickey." bhyslop's admin login works because `administrators_authorized_keys` ACL includes BUILTIN\Administrators which the impersonation token resolves through differently.
- **(Medium) `Match Group administrators` misfire.** Issue #1948 shows it blocking some non-admin Win10 cases even when membership is correct; issue #2254 shows it failing under domain configs; issue #2032 shows it failing on non-English locales (resolved by NAME string, not SID — `Match Group administrators` is broken on a Spanish system that has `Administradores`).
- **(Low) `secure_permissions()` parent-walk failure** on `C:\Users` or `C:\` — community sources don't document this walking above the home dir explicitly, and bhyslop's path traverses `C:\ProgramData\ssh\` through similar parent ACLs without issue.

**2. `secure_permissions()` parent walk:** Public Win32-OpenSSH wiki/docs do NOT document explicit traversal above the home directory. Trusted SIDs in the algorithm: SYSTEM, Administrators, file owner. Untrusted entries on the immediate `.ssh` and authorized_keys cause rejection — which you've cleared.

**3. `Match Group administrators` edge cases:**
- **Resolved by group NAME, not SID** (issue #2032). Non-English locale → rule fails to apply at all (or applies to wrong group).
- **Domain-joined hosts** with conflicting AD vs local "Administrators" mapping (issue #2254).
- A user accidentally in the local Administrators group will have authorized_keys looked up in `administrators_authorized_keys` (102B file), miss, and silently fail.

**4. Enable DEBUG3 file logging:**
In `C:\ProgramData\ssh\sshd_config`:
```
SyslogFacility LOCAL0
LogLevel DEBUG3
```
Then `Restart-Service sshd`. Logs land at `%ProgramData%\ssh\logs\sshd.log` (the `logs` dir you already see exists). Default `SyslogFacility AUTH` sends to Windows Event Log; `LOCAL0` switches to file.

**5. Cheap one-shot probes (no state mutation, no restart):**

**Recommended next probes:**
1. `Get-CimInstance Win32_Service -Filter "Name='sshd'" | Select StartName` — reveals whether sshd runs as `LocalSystem` or `NT SERVICE\sshd` (settles hypothesis A immediately).
2. `Get-LocalGroupMember -Group Administrators` and `Get-LocalGroupMember -Group Users` — confirms bujuw_user group membership (settles hypothesis B).
3. `(Get-Acl 'C:\Users\bujuw_user\.ssh\authorized_keys').Access | Format-Table IdentityReference,FileSystemRights` cross-referenced against the StartName from probe 1 — visualizes the access-mismatch directly.
4. `Get-WinEvent -ListLog OpenSSH/* | Format-Table` plus `Get-Content C:\ProgramData\ssh\logs\sshd.log -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Select-Object -Last 50` — confirms whether file-logging is already configured (and may surface prior preauth detail).
5. **If hypothesis A confirms:** add the sshd service principal to the ACL: `icacls 'C:\Users\bujuw_user\.ssh\authorized_keys' /grant 'NT SERVICE\sshd:R'` — narrowest fix. (Or run sshd as LocalSystem if it isn't.)

Sources:
- [Issue #1450: Won't read USER/.ssh/authorized_keys without sshd service user](https://github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH/issues/1450)
- [Issue #1948: Match Group administrators blocks public key auth](https://github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH/issues/1948)
- [Issue #2032: Wrong administrators group name on non-English locale](https://github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH/issues/2032)
- [Issue #2254: Match Group administrators fails in domain config](https://github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH/issues/2254)
- [Win32-OpenSSH Logging Facilities wiki](https://github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH/wiki/Logging-Facilities)
- [Microsoft Learn: Enable OpenSSH verbose logging](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/troubleshoot/windows-server/system-management-components/enable-openssh-verbose-logging)
- [Win32-OpenSSH security protection wiki](https://github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH/wiki/Security-protection-of-various-files-in-win32-openssh)
- [Microsoft Learn: Key-Based Authentication in OpenSSH for Windows](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-server/administration/openssh/openssh_keymanagement)</result>

</task-notification>

content.text:
Research is back, and there's a strong new hypothesis I hadn't considered.

**Top finding (high likelihood, fits the symptom exactly):**

sshd on Windows may be running as the virtual account `NT SERVICE\sshd`, NOT as `LocalSystem` (= NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM). These are distinct principals. Our authorized_keys ACL is `SYSTEM:F + bujuw_user:F` — so if sshd runs as `NT SERVICE\sshd`, it has no read access to the file, and silently drops at preauth before logging "Failed publickey." This is documented as Win32-OpenSSH issue #1450.

**Why bhyslop's admin login works regardless:** `administrators_authorized_keys` lives under `C:\ProgramData\ssh\` with default permissions that include the sshd service principal (or BUILTIN\Administrators, which the sshd service token resolves through differently than for non-admin user files). Different file, different ACL, no read-access problem.

**Other ranked hypotheses from the research:**
- **(Medium)** `Match Group administrators` misfires — known issues on non-English locales (group resolves by NAME not SID, e.g. fails on Spanish "Administradores"); domain-joined edge cases. Not likely on this English standalone host but cheap to rule out.
- **(Low)** `secure_permissions()` walking above the home dir — public Win32-OpenSSH docs don't actually document parent-walking above home, so this hypothesis (which we've been chasing for cycles) was probably wrong all along.

**Decisive cheap probe (one round-trip, no state mutation):**
```powershell
Get-CimInstance Win32_Service -Filter "Name='sshd'" | Select-Object StartName
```
- Returns `LocalSystem` → hypothesis A ruled out, look elsewhere.
- Returns `NT SERVICE\sshd` (or `LocalService`) → root cause confirmed; fix is one icacls call.

**Narrowest fix if hypothesis A confirms:**
```powershell
icacls 'C:\Users\bujuw_user\.ssh\authorized_keys' /grant 'NT SERVICE\sshd:R'
icacls 'C:\Users\bujuw_user\.ssh' /grant 'NT SERVICE\sshd:RX'
```
(grant traverse on `.ssh` plus read on the file). The `Match Group administrators` Default file works because its directory `C:\ProgramData\ssh` already grants the sshd service principal.

**Why this fits the symptom better than home-dir StrictModes:**
- Silent [preauth] close with no "Failed publickey" → sshd never opened authorized_keys, so never had a key to fail on. Matches `cannot read file` perfectly.
- StrictModes failure normally logs at LogLevel INFO with "Authentication refused: bad ownership or modes for file ..." — we see no such line. This was the missing piece.

**Operator decision territory:**

This is a meaningful new lead — the probe is one ssh round-trip, no state mutation, no fix application. It would settle whether the past several cycles' StrictModes-territory work was even on the right track.

Options:
1. **Single probe cycle** — add the `Get-CimInstance` probe to the validate failure path, run garrison-w once, read result. No fix attempt. Stops cleanly at evidence.
2. **Probe + speculative fix together** — run the probe AND grant `NT SERVICE\sshd:R/RX` on `.ssh` + authorized_keys in step 4. If probe shows `LocalSystem` we've added a harmless grant; if it shows `NT SERVICE\sshd` we've fixed it.
3. **Wrap pace as-is** — record the new hypothesis in the wrap summary; let the next pace pick it up fresh with this lead.

I'd note option 1 violates the spirit of "no more cycles," but it's a single probe, not iterative debugging — distinct from the cycle-1-through-7 trajectory. Option 2 has the same one-shot character but commits to a fix without ground truth. Option 3 is the cleanest stop.

Sources:
- [Win32-OpenSSH issue #1450 — sshd service user authorized_keys access](https://github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH/issues/1450)
- [Win32-OpenSSH issue #2032 — Match Group locale resolution](https://github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH/issues/2032)
- [Win32-OpenSSH wiki — Logging Facilities](https://github.com/PowerShell/Win32-OpenSSH/wiki/Logging-Facilities)

1	//axhob_operation
2	{bust_garrison_wsl}
3	
4	NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell,
5	for Windows nodes reached via the WSL distribution `rbtww-main`
6	(hardcoded in {bujb_jurisdiction}).
7	On Windows the workload identity inhabits *two namespaces*:
8	a Windows local user (created via `net.exe user /add`) provides the
9	SSH authentication boundary that Windows OpenSSH discovers, and a
10	Linux user inside `rbtww-main` (created via `useradd`) provides the
11	WSL execution context entered by the forced command. The Windows
12	user is the same shape minted by the c branch ({bust_garrison_cygwin}),
13	so Windows OpenSSH treats inbound auth identically; the WSL-side
14	`useradd` is layered on top so the SSH session lands as the named
15	WSL user (via `wsl.exe --user`) rather than the distro's default user.
16	Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
17	{bujb_workload_user} via the letter-agnostic
18	`zbujb_obliterate_workload` helper in {bujb_jurisdiction} — the
19	single greppable destroy verb. The covenant scope spans every
20	namespace a prior garrison of any letter could have written to: the
21	Windows SAM entry, the `C:\Users\<user>\` Windows profile directory,
22	the `C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\` Cygwin home, and the WSL-side
23	`/home/<user>` inside `rbtww-main`. Each step is idempotent on
24	absent state.
25	Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
26	admin authorized_keys; admin trust is established beforehand by
27	{busn_fenestrate}
28	on the Windows OpenSSH server, while sshd hardening inside the WSL
29	distribution remains operator-manual.
30	
31	//axhopt_typed_parameter
32	{burp_investiture}
33	
34	//axhoq_precondition
35	A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
36	referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.
37	
38	//axhoq_precondition
39	The WSL distribution `rbtww-main` (hardcoded as `BUJB_wsl_distribution`
40	in {bujb_jurisdiction}) is installed on the Windows host. Garrison-w
41	verifies this in a pre-flight after admin SSH succeeds; absence
42	surfaces a diagnostic naming the constant and emitting copy-paste
43	operator hints in the `tt/buw-jpS <investiture> '…'` shape rather
44	than letting an opaque `wsl.exe` failure escape downstream.
45	
46	//axhoq_precondition
47	{busn_fenestrate} has run for this {burp_investiture} so that
48	{burp_privileged_user} can authenticate by key alone using
49	{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host (the admin
50	entry point); the WSL session is then opened from that foothold.
51	
52	//axhoq_precondition
53	{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
54	station.
55	
56	//axhos_step
57	. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
58	{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host; key-auth
59	only. Failure here surfaces a missing or stale admin trust.
60	
61	//axhos_step
62	. *Pre-flight WSL distribution*: assert `BUJB_wsl_distribution` is
63	installed on the host (`wsl.exe --list --quiet` via the admin
64	session). Absence dies with a diagnostic naming the constant and
65	emitting `tt/buw-jpS` install hints; presence proceeds.
66	
67	//axhos_step
68	. *Obliterate workload*: invoke `zbujb_obliterate_workload`, the
69	letter-agnostic destroy helper. On Windows the helper executes a
70	single PowerShell session over admin SSH that purges the Windows
71	SAM entry (`net.exe user /delete`), the Windows profile directory
72	(`C:\Users\<user>\`), the Cygwin home (`C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\`),
73	and the WSL-side Linux user inside `rbtww-main`
74	(`wsl.exe --user root userdel -r`).
75	Each operation is idempotent on absent state.
76	
77	//axhos_step
78	. *Create workload*: provision {bujb_workload_user} in *both*
79	namespaces — the Windows user via `net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no
80	/active:yes` (matching the c branch) and the WSL Linux user via
81	`useradd --create-home --shell /bin/bash`.
82	
83	//axhos_step
84	. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
85	(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the Windows-side authorized_keys at
86	`/mnt/c/Users/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/authorized_keys` so Windows
87	OpenSSH discovers it natively (the SSH auth boundary is the Windows
88	user). The directory is created with `mkdir -p` and chmod 700; the
89	file is written and chmod 600. No POSIX `chown` is performed —
90	`/mnt/c/...` ownership is meaningless to Windows OpenSSH, which
91	inspects NTFS ACLs; the inherited NTFS ACL from the parent profile
92	directory is admin-owned and read-only for non-admins, satisfying
93	StrictModes.
94	The line carries the shell-letter `w` `command=` directive below —
95	locked spec content, mirrored at `BUJB_command_w` in
96	{bujb_jurisdiction}; `${BUJB_workload_user}` is substituted with the
97	canonical workload user at directive-emit time:
98	+
99	[listing]
100	----
101	command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user ${BUJB_workload_user} --exec /bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
102	----
103	
104	//axhos_step
105	. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the WSL-side
106	workload home at the shell-letter `w` destination path hardcoded in
107	{bujb_jurisdiction} (`/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519`),
108	so the Linux session reads it natively for outbound to GitHub.
109	
110	//axhos_step
111	. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command, verify
112	exit 0.
113	
114	//axhog_guarantee
115	On success the node has exactly one workload identity named
116	{bujb_workload_user} mirrored across the Windows user (auth boundary)
117	and the WSL Linux user (execution context inside `rbtww-main`).
118	
119	//axhoc_completion
120	Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
121	Non-zero on WSL distribution absence (pre-flight), admin SSH failure,
122	account-management failure in either namespace, key-write failure, or
123	workload validation failure.

file.content:
//axhob_operation
{bust_garrison_wsl}

NOTE: {busn_garrison} ceremony with WSL bash as the workload shell,
for Windows nodes reached via the WSL distribution `rbtww-main`
(hardcoded in {bujb_jurisdiction}).
On Windows the workload identity inhabits *two namespaces*:
a Windows local user (created via `net.exe user /add`) provides the
SSH authentication boundary that Windows OpenSSH discovers, and a
Linux user inside `rbtww-main` (created via `useradd`) provides the
WSL execution context entered by the forced command. The Windows
user is the same shape minted by the c branch ({bust_garrison_cygwin}),
so Windows OpenSSH treats inbound auth identically; the WSL-side
`useradd` is layered on top so the SSH session lands as the named
WSL user (via `wsl.exe --user`) rather than the distro's default user.
Workload-only: garrison destroys any prior workload account named
{bujb_workload_user} via the letter-agnostic
`zbujb_obliterate_workload` helper in {bujb_jurisdiction} — the
single greppable destroy verb. The covenant scope spans every
namespace a prior garrison of any letter could have written to: the
Windows SAM entry, the `C:\Users\<user>\` Windows profile directory,
the `C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\` Cygwin home, and the WSL-side
`/home/<user>` inside `rbtww-main`. Each step is idempotent on
absent state.
Garrison never reads or writes any `sshd_config` and never modifies
admin authorized_keys; admin trust is established beforehand by
{busn_fenestrate}
on the Windows OpenSSH server, while sshd hardening inside the WSL
distribution remains operator-manual.

//axhopt_typed_parameter
{burp_investiture}

//axhoq_precondition
A {burp_regime} profile exists at the given {burp_investiture} and its
referenced {burn_regime} profile is reachable.

//axhoq_precondition
The WSL distribution `rbtww-main` (hardcoded as `BUJB_wsl_distribution`
in {bujb_jurisdiction}) is installed on the Windows host. Garrison-w
verifies this in a pre-flight after admin SSH succeeds; absence
surfaces a diagnostic naming the constant and emitting copy-paste
operator hints in the `tt/buw-jpS <investiture> '…'` shape rather
than letting an opaque `wsl.exe` failure escape downstream.

//axhoq_precondition
{busn_fenestrate} has run for this {burp_investiture} so that
{burp_privileged_user} can authenticate by key alone using
{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host (the admin
entry point); the WSL session is then opened from that foothold.

//axhoq_precondition
{burp_workload_key_file} is unencrypted and exists on the operator's
station.

//axhos_step
. *Open admin SSH*: connect as {burp_privileged_user} using
{burp_privileged_key_file} into the Windows OpenSSH host; key-auth
only. Failure here surfaces a missing or stale admin trust.

//axhos_step
. *Pre-flight WSL distribution*: assert `BUJB_wsl_distribution` is
installed on the host (`wsl.exe --list --quiet` via the admin
session). Absence dies with a diagnostic naming the constant and
emitting `tt/buw-jpS` install hints; presence proceeds.

//axhos_step
. *Obliterate workload*: invoke `zbujb_obliterate_workload`, the
letter-agnostic destroy helper. On Windows the helper executes a
single PowerShell session over admin SSH that purges the Windows
SAM entry (`net.exe user /delete`), the Windows profile directory
(`C:\Users\<user>\`), the Cygwin home (`C:\cygwin64\home\<user>\`),
and the WSL-side Linux user inside `rbtww-main`
(`wsl.exe --user root userdel -r`).
Each operation is idempotent on absent state.

//axhos_step
. *Create workload*: provision {bujb_workload_user} in *both*
namespaces — the Windows user via `net.exe user /add /passwordreq:no
/active:yes` (matching the c branch) and the WSL Linux user via
`useradd --create-home --shell /bin/bash`.

//axhos_step
. *Place workload trust*: write the workload pubkey
(derived via `ssh-keygen -y`) to the Windows-side authorized_keys at
`/mnt/c/Users/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/authorized_keys` so Windows
OpenSSH discovers it natively (the SSH auth boundary is the Windows
user). The directory is created with `mkdir -p` and chmod 700; the
file is written and chmod 600. No POSIX `chown` is performed —
`/mnt/c/...` ownership is meaningless to Windows OpenSSH, which
inspects NTFS ACLs; the inherited NTFS ACL from the parent profile
directory is admin-owned and read-only for non-admins, satisfying
StrictModes.
The line carries the shell-letter `w` `command=` directive below —
locked spec content, mirrored at `BUJB_command_w` in
{bujb_jurisdiction}; `${BUJB_workload_user}` is substituted with the
canonical workload user at directive-emit time:
+
[listing]
----
command="C:/Windows/System32/wsl.exe --distribution rbtww-main --user ${BUJB_workload_user} --exec /bin/bash -lc \"$SSH_ORIGINAL_COMMAND\"",no-port-forwarding,no-X11-forwarding,no-agent-forwarding
----

//axhos_step
. *Plant workload key*: copy {burp_workload_key_file} to the WSL-side
workload home at the shell-letter `w` destination path hardcoded in
{bujb_jurisdiction} (`/home/${BUJB_workload_user}/.ssh/id_ed25519`),
so the Linux session reads it natively for outbound to GitHub.

//axhos_step
. *Validate round-trip*: SSH as workload, run a no-op command, verify
exit 0.

//axhog_guarantee
On success the node has exactly one workload identity named
{bujb_workload_user} mirrored across the Windows user (auth boundary)
and the WSL Linux user (execution context inside `rbtww-main`).

//axhoc_completion
Exit 0 when the round-trip validation succeeds.
Non-zero on WSL distribution absence (pre-flight), admin SSH failure,
account-management failure in either namespace, key-write failure, or
workload validation failure.
